definition: Liquidity within a defined price range, primarily unfilled fair value gaps (FVGs), equal highs/lows, and equilibrium. After external liquidity (swing highs/lows) is swept, price targets the nearest internal liquidity before continuing the cycle. Internal liquidity serves as the first take-profit target and is distinct from external liquidity.
identification_rules: Define the price range by marking a clear external swing high and swing low on the relevant time frame.

Within that range, identify unfilled fair value gaps (three-candle imbalances or gaps between wicks) as primary internal liquidity; optionally include the range's 50% equilibrium, prior sub-ranges (e.g., daily open), and equal highs/lows.
Confirm the level is within the range, not beyond the swing high or swing low (which would classify it as external liquidity).
After price sweeps external liquidity (a swing high or low), the nearest unfilled FVG inside the range becomes the next draw as internal liquidity.
After price trades into the FVG (above its low or below its high), expect the cycle to continue toward the opposite external swing.
If no FVG exists within the range, internal liquidity is undefined; wait for a gap to form before projecting the cycle.
For additional confirmation, compare correlated assets: if one reaches into the FVG while the other does not, that SMT divergence signals a potential reversal at the internal liquidity level.
Use top-down analysis: establish the IRL-to-ERL direction on a higher time frame, then drop to a lower time frame where the HTF FVG boundary acts as a swing point for refined entry.
When price fails to displace beyond the range and returns inside, internal liquidity (equilibrium, FVG) becomes the primary draw instead of external levels.

Identify the internal high and low of the FVG (the high of the first candle and low of the third candle for bullish FVG, or vice versa for bearish); price tapping into the gap is equivalent to taking internal liquidity.
trading_implication: After price sweeps external liquidity (e.g., London session purges a swing low), internal liquidity — such as a fair value gap or equal highs/lows inside the range — becomes the first take-profit target. Traders should not skip internal liquidity when setting targets. Use top-down analysis: establish IRL-to-ERL direction on a higher time frame, then enter on a lower time frame within the FVG using order blocks or breaker blocks. SMT divergences at FVG boundaries confirm reversals. In fail-to-displace conditions, internal levels become primary targets, favoring mean reversion over breakout strategies.
common_mistakes: Targeting equilibrium or a higher HTF level before accounting for internal liquidity as the first, nearer target.
Confusing internal liquidity (inside the range) with external liquidity (beyond swing highs/lows), leading to incorrect target sequencing.
Failing to recognize that an FVG's high and low are swing-level points, so a tap into the gap is equivalent to taking internal liquidity.
Omitting correlated asset SMT divergence confirmation when price reaches IRL.
Expecting price to always retrace to the FVG before the next external sweep; it may continue without returning.
Attempting to project IRL-to-ERL cycles when no fair value gap has formed inside the range.
Ignoring whether price respects or displaces through the FVG (inversion), which alters the directional bias.
Trading range breakouts when price consistently fails to displace and returns to internal levels.
Trading aggressively during consolidation/Seek and Destroy environments rather than recognizing them as low-probability.
Expecting large directional moves when the daily range has already been reached early, leading to consolidation.
Assuming price always cycles perfectly between internal and external liquidity; price may skip certain areas or not fully fill FVGs.
Applying the concept on a single timeframe without considering multi-timeframe relationships.
Analyzing only one asset without comparing correlated assets to identify true SMT.
Confusing positively and negatively correlated pairs' expected structures (positive should move together, negative opposite).
Failing to correctly mark the internal high and low of the FVG, leading to inaccurate comparison of divergences between assets.
Mistaking a single prominent swing high/low (old high/low) for internal range liquidity; those are external range liquidity.
Marking internal liquidity before a fair value gap has actually formed.
Considering an FVG as a valid internal liquidity target before external liquidity has been swept (price has not closed back inside the range).
Continuing to treat an FVG as valid internal liquidity after price has inverted through it (inversion fair value gap).
merge_notes: School conflict: 3 ICT + 1 SMC + 1 SMC; picked ICT by majority. One SMC card defined IRL as only FVGs, while ICT cards include equal highs/lows and equilibrium; unified card includes all. The Foundation card adds nuance about FVG internal structure (high/low boundaries) and requirement that external liquidity be swept before IRL is valid; these are preserved.
